attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic seeking advice after multiple gallstones were found incidentally on ultrasound scanning during her recent pregnancy. She has never experienced abdominal pain or other gallstone-related symptoms. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She works as an accountant.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her BMI is 32 kg/m². Abdominal examination is unremarkable.

The ultrasound report reads:

Multiple hyperechoic masses located in the body of the gallbladder, with posterior acoustic shadowing consistent with gallstone disease. The largest mass is 4 mm. There is no dilatation of the biliary tree.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Advise weight loss prior to surgery
attributes.explanation:
This patient has asymptomatic gallstones discovered incidentally during pregnancy. The most appropriate management is expectant management, which involves educating the patient about symptoms of biliary colic and advising her to seek medical attention if these develop. The majority of patients with asymptomatic gallstones remain symptom-free and do not require intervention.

Elective cholecystectomy is not indicated for asymptomatic gallstones unless specific risk factors are present, such as sickle cell disease or stones larger than 3 cm (due to increased risk of gallbladder cancer). This patient's stones are small (4 mm) and she has no risk factors warranting prophylactic surgery.

Cholecystectomy prior to next pregnancy is not indicated as there is no evidence supporting prophylactic surgery in asymptomatic patients planning pregnancy. While pregnancy is a risk factor for gallstone formation, most pregnant women with gallstones remain asymptomatic.

Advising weight loss prior to surgery is inappropriate as surgery is not indicated. Moreover, rapid weight loss actually increases the risk of gallstone formation due to altered cholesterol metabolism.

Gallstone dissolution therapy with ursodeoxycholic acid is only considered for symptomatic patients who are not surgical candidates. It is not indicated for asymptomatic gallstones.

The natural history of asymptomatic gallstones is generally benign, with only about 20% of patients developing symptoms over 20 years. The risk of complications (such as acute cholecystitis, pancreatitis, or cholangitis) is even lower at approximately 1-2% per year.

Prophylactic cholecystectomy may be performed in patients with sickle cell disease, for whom there is a high risk of complications associated with pigmented gallstones. It may also be considered in patients with ultrasound findings conferring an increased risk of gallbladder cancer, including gallbladder polyps and stones > 3 cm.
learningPoints: Asymptomatic gallstones generally do not require operative management.
Rapid weight loss predisposes to risk of gallstone formation.
references: Warttig S, Ward S, Rogers G; Guideline Development Group. Diagnosis and management of gallstone disease: summary of NICE guidance. BMJ. 2014 Oct 30;349:g6241.
Gutt C, Schläfer S, Lammert F. The Treatment of Gallstone Disease. Dtsch Arztebl Int. 2020;117(9):148-158. doi:10.3238/arztebl.2020.0148
